🌐 Organisation Intelligence Overview
Claude AI
Import data to generate org-level AI overview…

Overall Pulse & Engagement Health

Organisation average attrition risk is 58%. Industry benchmarks suggest healthy organisations operate at 10–15% annual attrition. Current risk levels are significantly elevated and require immediate leadership intervention. 3 employees are currently classified as high risk (≥65%), representing 0% of the workforce.

Key Attrition Risk Trends

669 employees are below the 0.8 compa-ratio threshold — compensation below market is consistently the top attrition driver globally. 438 employees carry Red pulse flags — direct HRBP behavioural observation has high predictive validity. 919 employees have not been promoted in over 3 years, increasing flight risk significantly in knowledge-economy roles.

Recommended Retention Interventions

1. Compensation correction: Review all employees below 0.85 compa-ratio for salary adjustments in the next review cycle. 2. Career pathway conversations: Mandate manager-led career conversations for all employees with 3+ years since last promotion. 3. Pulse-driven check-ins: All Red-pulse employees should receive an HRBP 1:1 within 2 weeks. 4. Leader effectiveness: Low leader pulse scores affect entire team engagement — targeted coaching for managers below 50/100. 5. Internal mobility programme: Structured role rotation reduces tenure stagnation and is proven to improve retention by 30–40%.

Engagement Summary
Divya Agarwal in Data Science & AI — Engagement: 60/100 (Moderately Engaged), ML Attrition: 65% (High Risk). Current tenure: 6y 11m. Compa-ratio 1.16 — at or above market. Risk areas: Disciplinary Record, Increment vs Team, Last Promotion. Strengths: Commute Distance & Compa-Ratio. Immediate manager and HRBP attention required. Consider this employee a near-term flight risk.
Manager Action Guide
Claude AI
Preparing recommendations…
1. Prioritise a deeper, agenda-free 1:1 this week. HRBP has noted: "Strong NLP work but feels siloed from product decisions. Wants closer integration with PM team.". Move beyond project updates — ask directly what's energising them and what feels heavy right now. Employees who feel genuinely heard are significantly less likely to begin exploring external options.
2. Have an honest career horizon conversation. Divya Agarwal hasn't had a promotion or role change in 5 years — one of the strongest predictors of passive disengagement. Discuss what "progress" looks like for them, name any gaps explicitly, and commit to one visible action this quarter such as a stretch project, cross-functional exposure, or a skills development plan.
3. Check in on energy levels and workload sustainability. 6 absence days last quarter often signals burnout accumulating below the surface. Ask openly about pace and pressure points — and whether the current workload feels manageable. Adjusting project allocation or offering a short recovery sprint can prevent a more serious disengagement.
4. Make sure strong performance is visibly recognised — especially given the attrition risk signals. High-performing employees who feel underappreciated leave quietly and quickly. Mention their contributions explicitly in team forums, give direct attribution on outcomes they own, and consider whether they could take on a visible cross-team role that builds their profile.
Risk Signals

Import data to generate org-level insights…

Overall Pulse & Engagement Health

Organisation average attrition risk is 58%. Industry benchmarks suggest healthy organisations operate at 10–15% annual attrition. Current risk levels are significantly elevated and require immediate leadership intervention. 3 employees are currently classified as high risk (≥65%), representing 0% of the workforce.

Key Attrition Risk Trends

669 employees are below the 0.8 compa-ratio threshold — compensation below market is consistently the top attrition driver globally. 438 employees carry Red pulse flags — direct HRBP behavioural observation has high predictive validity. 919 employees have not been promoted in over 3 years, increasing flight risk significantly in knowledge-economy roles.

Recommended Retention Interventions

1. Compensation correction: Review all employees below 0.85 compa-ratio for salary adjustments in the next review cycle. 2. Career pathway conversations: Mandate manager-led career conversations for all employees with 3+ years since last promotion. 3. Pulse-driven check-ins: All Red-pulse employees should receive an HRBP 1:1 within 2 weeks. 4. Leader effectiveness: Low leader pulse scores affect entire team engagement — targeted coaching for managers below 50/100. 5. Internal mobility programme: Structured role rotation reduces tenure stagnation and is proven to improve retention by 30–40%.

Key Opportunity Areas
💰
Below-Market Compensation (669)
669 employees have a compa-ratio below 0.80. This is the highest-impact, fastest-acting attrition driver. These employees should be flagged for priority review in the next compensation cycle.
Compensation→ See market practice
🚀
Career Stagnation Risk (1226)
1226 employees have been without a promotion or role change for 3 or more years. Stagnation beyond 3 years is a leading indicator of passive disengagement and eventual resignation.
Career→ See market practice
🔴
Red Pulse Observations (438)
438 employees are rated Red by their HRBP. These are direct human observations of disengagement, carrying the highest predictive validity of any factor. All require an active retention plan within the next 30 days.
Engagement→ See market practice
📅
High Absenteeism Pattern (473)
473 employees have 8 or more leave days in the last quarter. At this level, absenteeism is a reliable indicator of burnout or disengagement building below the surface.
Wellness→ See market practice
👥
Low Leader Pulse Scores (615)
615 team leaders have a pulse score below 50/100. Gallup research attributes 70% of team engagement variance to the direct manager — low leader scores will cascade to team attrition if unaddressed.
Leadership→ See market practice

🏆 Market Practices & Recommended InterventionsIndustry best practices · evidence-based retention strategies
Based on your organisation's identified risk areas, the following market-validated practices are most relevant. These reflect approaches used by leading organisations to address similar attrition and engagement challenges.
💰 Compensation Correction Strategy
Leading organisations conduct quarterly compa-ratio audits and set a policy floor of 0.85 (85% of market benchmark) as the minimum acceptable compensation level. Employees below 0.80 are flagged for immediate review in the next compensation cycle. Research from Mercer and Willis Towers Watson consistently shows that below-market pay is the single highest-impact, fastest-acting attrition driver — corrections here produce the most immediate risk reduction.
Quarterly compa audits0.85 policy floorOff-cycle corrections for critical talentPay equity reviews
🚀 Career Progression Frameworks
Organisations with structured career frameworks — clearly defined role levels, skills matrices, and promotion criteria — show 20–35% lower attrition among employees in the 2–5 year tenure band. Best practice involves a formal annual career conversation with every employee, documented progression plans, and visible internal mobility pathways. The absence of perceived career growth is the second most-cited reason for resignation after compensation.
Skills matrices by levelAnnual progression reviewsStretch assignmentsInternal mobility policy
👥 Manager Capability Development
Gallup's State of the Global Workplace research attributes 70% of team engagement variance to the direct manager. High-performing HR organisations invest in structured manager enablement: monthly manager effectiveness scores, mandatory coaching skills training, and a "manager NPS" metric tracked alongside team attrition. Managers in the bottom quartile of leader pulse scores are paired with HR business partners for targeted support before attrition risk escalates.
Manager NPS trackingCoaching skills programmeHRBP pairing for at-risk managers360 feedback
🏠 Flexible Work & Commute Policies
For employees with commute distances above 30km, leading organisations have implemented hybrid work models (3 days in-office, 2 remote) that reduce effective commute burden by 40%. Where full flexibility is not possible, transport allowances, staggered start times, and co-working space subsidies are used to reduce friction. Microsoft's Work Trend Index found that flexible work is now among the top 3 factors in employment decisions for employees across all demographics.
Hybrid work policyTransport allowanceStaggered hoursCo-working access
🔄 Internal Mobility Programmes
Research by LinkedIn shows that employees who make an internal move within 2 years of joining stay an average of 2x longer than those who don't. Organisations with formal internal mobility programmes — internal job boards, manager-supported lateral moves, and cross-functional project opportunities — see 25–30% improvement in engagement scores among employees who have been in role for 3+ years without a vertical promotion.
Internal job boardLateral move supportCross-functional projectsTalent marketplace
⭐ Recognition & Non-Monetary Retention
Deloitte's Human Capital Trends report found that recognition programmes correlate with a 31% reduction in voluntary turnover. Best-practice organisations operate multi-channel recognition (peer-to-peer, manager-led, org-wide), set a target of at least one meaningful recognition moment per employee per quarter, and tie recognition visibility to performance cycles. For employees below the engagement threshold, targeted recognition investments often produce faster engagement recovery than structural changes.
Peer recognition platformManager-led recognitionQuarterly awardsValues-based recognition
📋 Prioritised Action PlanData-driven priorities based on your organisation
PriorityActionTarget PopulationTimelineImpact
1Compensation review669 employees below 0.80 compa-ratioNext comp cycle (immediate for critical talent)High
